name: Yamunacharya
dates_notes: Sectarian chronicle dating only; long lifespan in tradition is suspect.

lineage_polemical.note: The Saṃvit-Siddhi mounts the principal Viśiṣṭādvaita refutation of Yogācāra: the very thesis of vijñapti-mātra requires that cognition be self-luminous, but cognition's content must point beyond itself, and the bāhya-artha is therefore a presupposition of the very position that denies it. Yāmuna reads Vasubandhu's Viṃśatikā as the canonical statement of the position to be refuted.

The Saṃvit-Siddhi targets the broader Yogācāra ālaya-vijñāna apparatus: a momentary substrate cannot ground the cross-moment continuity of karmic effect that the Yogācāra appeals to it for, and an enduring ātman is therefore required after all. The structural target is the Mahāyāna-Saṅgraha doctrine that Asaṅga founded.

The Āgama-Prāmāṇya is the major defence of the Pāñcarātra-Saṃhitās from within Viśiṣṭādvaita: Yāmuna re-reads Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.42–45 not as a refutation of Pāñcarātra-as-such but as a refutation of one specific (and rejectable) reading of the vyūha-production, and re-establishes the canonical authority of the Saṃhitās for the Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition.

core_thesis: Yāmuna is the first Śrīvaiṣṇava to give Viśiṣṭādvaita the form of an argued philosophical system; where Nāthamuni left a bilingual liturgical-philosophical corpus, Yāmuna writes Sanskrit *siddhi*-treatises in formal *pūrvapakṣa-siddhānta* register. The architecture is fixed in three *Siddhi*-treatises and a Pāñcarātra defence. Brahman — Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī — is a qualified unity whose body (*śarīra*) is the totality of conscious selves (*cit*) and non-conscious matter (*acit*), each really distinct yet inseparable (*apṛthak-siddha*) from him as their inner controller (*antaryāmin*). The *jīva* is an atomic, eternal *jñātṛ* (knower), not a momentary cognition-stream and not pure unqualified *cit*. The *Saṃvit-Siddhi* mounts the school's epistemology: awareness is *dharmi-bhūta-jñāna*, a self-revelatory (*svayam-prakāśa*) substance directed always at a content; it cannot be its own object in the sense the proto-Advaitin requires, since *svayam-prakāśa* and *viṣayatva* are distinct relations. Against this Yāmuna's destructive argument runs: if consciousness disclosed itself as its own object, every cognition would be a self-cognition; but on first-order experience cognitions are directed at pots, blue patches, sounds, not at themselves; therefore self-disclosure (*svayam-prakāśa*) cannot be construed as object-disclosure. The same epistemology yields *yathārtha-khyāti*: every cognition has a real object, error is partial apprehension rather than the cognising of a non-entity. Two further moves complete the position. The *Āgama-Prāmāṇya* defends Pāñcarātra revelation as continuous with *śruti-prāmāṇya* against Mīmāṃsaka exclusionism. And the *Stotra-Ratna* elevates *prapatti* — surrender by the *ākiñcana*, the self stripped of all resource — to recognised *upāya*-status alongside *bhakti-yoga*.

engaged_works.title: Siddhi-Trayam
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored triad of Siddhi-treatises (Ātma-, Īśvara-, Saṃvit-Siddhi).
engaged_works.summary: A triad of *prakaraṇa*-treatises — *Ātma-Siddhi*, *Īśvara-Siddhi*, *Saṃvit-Siddhi* — establishing in turn the *jīva*, the personal *Īśvara*, and self-luminous awareness. The *Ātma-Siddhi* (verse 3) fixes the self with five predicates: distinct from body, senses, mind, breath, and cognition (*dehendriyamanaḥprāṇadhībhyo 'nyaḥ*); self-manifest (*ananyasādhana*); eternal; pervasive in its essence yet distinct in each bodily field (*pratikṣetram*); and intrinsically blissful. Each predicate is calibrated against an opponent. *Anyaḥ* refutes Cārvāka body-reduction; *ananyasādhana* refutes the proto-Advaitin who would make the self knowable only through a second cognition; *nityaḥ* refutes Buddhist momentariness; *pratikṣetram* refutes a single-self monism; *svataḥ sukhī* refutes a self that depends on adventitious karma for its essential character. The *Īśvara-Siddhi* (verses 16–18) mounts a four-step *anumāna* for a personal *Īśvara*: the world is perceptible because it is an entity (like a pot); it follows a single will because it is non-conscious (like one's own body, governed by a single agent); conscious agents act under one director because their action depends on embodiment (like skin and the rest); therefore the disputed world has one principal person, since it is a conscious-non-conscious complex (like a polity under one king). The *Saṃvit-Siddhi* defends *dharmi-bhūta-jñāna*. Its destructive contribution is the case against the proto-Advaitin claim that consciousness is self-revelatory in the strong sense of disclosing itself as its own object. Yāmuna's argument: *svayam-prakāśa* (self-luminosity) and *viṣayatva* (object-status) are distinct relations; cognition is directed at content, not at itself; were it directed at itself, the knower-known structure would collapse into a single relatum and there would be no cognising at all. Verses 31–35 then turn this against the Advaitic reading of *advitīyam brahma*: the world's reality is established by its own *pramāṇa*-force and corroborated by *śruti*; *advitīya* therefore means only that no second equal-or-superior to Brahman exists, not that the manifold is unreal. The triad is the philosophical bedrock on which Rāmānuja's *Śrī-Bhāṣya* stands.

engaged_works.title: Agama-Pramanya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored defense of Pāñcarātra agama-validity.
engaged_works.summary: A *prakaraṇa* defending the scriptural authority (*prāmāṇya*) of the Pāñcarātra *saṃhitā*-corpus against the Mīmāṃsaka objection that, being non-Vedic and authored by a personal Bhagavān, the Pāñcarātra cannot count as *śruti* and its initiates fall outside the four *varṇa*-orders. Yāmuna's reply has three moves. First, on authorship: the Pāñcarātra is the utterance of Nārāyaṇa, who is the source of the Veda itself; an utterance of the *vedasya kartā* is not weaker in authority than the Veda. Second, on content: the Pāñcarātra prescriptions do not contradict *śruti* but determine what *śruti* leaves undetermined regarding Vaiṣṇava liturgy, and so stand to *śruti* as supplementary smṛti rather than as rival. Third, on social order: Pāñcarātra initiates (*bhāgavata*-s) are not a fifth *varṇa* outside Vedic order but qualified *brāhmaṇa*-s practising a particular *kalpa*. The work secured the institutional legitimacy of the Śrīvaiṣṇava temple-cultus as Vaidika orthodoxy. Philosophically, it bequeaths to Rāmānuja and Vedānta Deśika a layered theory of *prāmāṇya* on which scriptural authority can be carried by texts other than the four Vedas without descending into sectarian innovation.

engaged_works.title: Stotra-Ratna
engaged_works.summary: A sixty-five-verse Sanskrit hymn to Nārāyaṇa, and the literary template for the entire Śrīvaiṣṇava *stotra*-tradition. Its devotional surface enacts the doctrinal architecture of the *Siddhi-Trayam*. Nārāyaṇa is addressed as Brahman *saviśeṣa* (qualified by *ananta-kalyāṇa-guṇa*, infinite auspicious attributes), as *antaryāmin* of the totality of *cit* and *acit*, and as the sole refuge of the *ākiñcana* — the self stripped of all resource. The doctrinally load-bearing verses are 22–24. Verse 22 (*na dharma-niṣṭho 'smi, na cātmavedī, na bhaktimāṃs tvaccaraṇāravinde / akiñcano 'nanyagatiḥ śaraṇya, tvatpādamūlaṃ śaraṇaṃ prapadye*) declares the surrendering self three times in the negative — not steadfast in *dharma*, not a knower of the self, not possessed of devotion — before the act of taking refuge. The negation is structural: *prapatti* is not the achievement of a qualified self but the act of one who has nothing to bring. Verses 23–24 then give the temporal frame: confessed moral failure across countless lives, karmic fruition arriving, the Lord found at last as a shore (*kūlam iva*) for one sinking in the ocean of *bhava*. The selection prevents cherry-picking of the famous verse 22 in isolation: surrender is embedded in karmic vulnerability and in the Lord's compassionate initiative. The hymn matters because it shows the Yāmuna of the *Siddhi*-treatises and the Yāmuna of the liturgy expressing one position: *bhakti* is not subordinate to *jñāna* but is *jñāna* in its mature relational form, terminating in surrender to the divine person.

engaged_works.title: Catuh-Shloki
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored four-verse hymn to Śrī.
engaged_works.summary: A four-verse Sanskrit hymn to Śrī (Lakṣmī), foundational for Śrīvaiṣṇava *Śrī-tattva* — the doctrine of the goddess as eternal, inseparable consort and mediatrix of Nārāyaṇa. The four verses fix four points. First, Śrī's eternity and ontological inseparability from Nārāyaṇa, together with her personal distinctness as a relatum within the divine pair (*Catuḥ-Ślokī* 1, *kāntas te puruṣottamaḥ*). Second, her status as universal mother (*jaganmātṛ*) whose mercy (*kṛpā*) is the operative principle of grace (*Catuḥ-Ślokī* 2). Third, her role as *puruṣakāra* — the one who solicits the Lord's compassion on behalf of the surrendered *jīva* (*Catuḥ-Ślokī* 3, *yasyāḥ kaṭākṣa-mātreṇa*). Fourth, the surrendering self's place at her feet as the proper attitude of *prapatti* (*Catuḥ-Ślokī* 4). The text becomes doctrinally load-bearing two centuries later: the Vaḍakalai-Tenkalai split is partly a quarrel over its construal. The Tenkalai (Pillai-Lokācārya, *Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa*; Maṇavāḷa Māmunigaḷ's commentary) reads it as making Śrī herself an *upāya* in concert with Nārāyaṇa; the Vaḍakalai (Vedānta Deśika, *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra* and *Catuḥ-Ślokī-Bhāṣya*) reads it as fixing her as *puruṣakāra* — mediating advocate enabling Nārāyaṇa's grace — without making her an independent means. Both schools accept the text as canonical; their divergence is exegetical.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
dehendriyamanaḥprāṇadhībhyo 'nyo 'nanyasādhanaḥ /
nityo vyāpī pratikṣetram ātmā bhinnaḥ svataḥ sukhī //
key_passages.text_critical_note: The transmitted GRETIL text reads `ananyasādhanaḥ`. The attached commentary later rejects an older printed reading `hānasvabhāva` and explains `ananyasādhanaḥ` as self-manifestness (`ananyena svena sādhanaṃ siddhiḥ prakāśaḥ yasya`).

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: dehebhyaḥ indriyebhyaḥ manasaḥ prāṇebhyaḥ dhībhyāś ca
samasa_vigrahas.note: The verse compresses the non-self series into one ablative dvandva.
Under the commentary's explanation, this marks the self as not needing a second revealer.
Distributive: 'with respect to each field/body'.

karaka_structure.note: Subject of the implied copula.
The self is distinguished away from this non-self aggregate.
Distributive locus: each bodily field.

verb_modality.note: Nominal verse with an implied present copula.

key_passages.english_close: [The self is] other than body, senses, mind, vital breaths, and cognitions; and it does not need any other [instrument to manifest itself]. The self is eternal, pervasive, distinct in each bodily field (kṣetra), and by nature blissful.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is not just a slogan but Yāmuna's own compact thesis-verse for the self: it fixes the self against body-mind reduction, against momentariness, and against pure-consciousness monism in one place. It fairly represents the Ātma-Siddhi because the surrounding commentary unpacks exactly these predicates as the work's programmatic claims.
key_passages.source_edition: Atma Siddhi, Yamuna, GRETIL plaintext (materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_atma_siddhi_gretil.txt; corpus_meta.tsv).

samasa_vigrahas.note: The world's constitution as a conscious-nonconscious complex.

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: ekaḥ rājā yasmin deśe saḥ iva
samasa_vigrahas.note: A polity structured by one ruler.

karaka_structure.note: Explicit subject of `kurvate`.
Instrumental of the one presiding agent.
Object performed.
Each inferential clause predicates a property of the world.

verb_modality.note: Implied copula in the nominal predications.

key_passages.english_close: That [world] is perceptible (pratyakṣa). [Why?] Because it is an object of knowledge, because it is an entity — just like a pot and the like. This [world] follows one will. [Why?] Because it is non-conscious, just like one's own body. All conscious beings, being controlled by one, perform action. [Why?] Because their agency depends on relation to a body, just like skin and the rest [perform action under the will of the embodied self]. The disputed world has one principal person (eka-pradhāna-puruṣa). [Why?] Because it consists of conscious and non-conscious components, just like a land with a single king.
key_passages.why_this_passage: These verses are useful precisely because they are not merely a devotional assertion: they condense Yāmuna's inferential strategy for a personal Lord who orders both conscious and non-conscious reality. That makes them a fair representative of the Īśvara-Siddhi's project of arguing toward a real, governing Īśvara rather than simply presupposing him.
key_passages.source_edition: Isvara Siddhi, Yamuna, GRETIL plaintext (materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_isvara_siddhi_gretil.txt; corpus_meta.tsv).

key_passages.english_close: Therefore the real existence (sadbhāva) of the prapañca is not negated by the non-duality śruti. It is established by the force of its own pramāṇa. And it is also approved by śruti. Thus, this is said to be the meaning of the scriptural statement "Brahman is one-without-a-second". There did not exist, does not exist, and will not exist anything countable as a second [to Brahman] — whether equal to it or superior to it.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: without a second in the sense of no equal or superior counterpart

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is one of the clearest places where Yāmuna states that Upaniṣadic non-duality does not cancel the world's reality but only denies any equal or superior second to Brahman. It fairly represents his position because it is not an isolated flourish: it explicitly ties ontology to the force of ordinary and scriptural pramāṇas, which is exactly how the Saṃvit-Siddhi argues.
key_passages.source_edition: Samvit Siddhi, Yamuna, GRETIL plaintext based on Roque Mesquita's 1988 edition (materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_samvit_siddhi_gretil.txt; corpus_meta.tsv).

samasa_vigrahas.note: One established in dharma.
Knower of the self.
Lotus-foot; locative of devotional focus.
Possessing no resource.

The base of the Lord's feet as refuge.

Locus for the denied possession of bhakti.

Temporal locus for the cry of helplessness.
Vocatives intensifying dependence on the Lord.
Genitive relation: the speaker as recipient-vessel of compassion.

verb_modality.note: Identification of the Lord as the discovered shore.

key_passages.english_close: I am not one steadfast in dharma, nor a knower of the self, nor one possessed of devotion at your lotus-feet. Possessing nothing, with no other refuge, O refuge-giver, I take refuge in the root of your feet. There is no blameworthy deed in the world which I have not performed thousands of times. I, at the time of karmic fruition, O Mukunda, now cry out before you, bereft of recourse. For me, long sinking in the depth of the ocean of becoming, O Endless One, you are like a shore at last found; and by you too, O Lord, this unsurpassed vessel of compassion has now been found.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: refuge, the act and state of resorting
I take refuge; the verbal core of prapatti

key_passages.why_this_passage: Verse 22 is famous, but taking 22-24 together prevents cherry-picking: the surrender is embedded in confessed moral failure, karmic vulnerability, and the Lord's compassionate initiative. That is exactly how Yāmuna presents prapatti in the Stotra-Ratna, so this selection fairly represents the work rather than isolating a slogan.
key_passages.source_edition: Stotra Ratna, Yamuna, GRETIL plaintext (materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_stotra_ratna_gretil.txt; corpus_meta.tsv).
